narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 58.9% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students who are likely to succeed in passing the bar exam.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance in Year 1 and Year 3, represented by your decile rankings, were significant contributors. Both of these were lower than the average, which pushed the prediction towards a potential failure. Additionally, your LSAT score was below the average, which also contributed to the model's decision in a similar direction. Your family's income bracket, categorized as lower-middle, was another factor that influenced the prediction towards failure, although it was less impactful than your academic performance and LSAT score.

Your racial background as a Black student was also considered, but it had a smaller influence compared to the academic factors. On the positive side, your undergraduate GPA was close to the average, which slightly countered the negative influences. Your gender and full-time status had minimal impact on the prediction.

Given this analysis, you might consider focusing on improving your LSAT score and academic performance in future endeavors. These are areas where you have the potential to make changes that could positively influence your chances of passing the bar exam. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive measure of your abilities or potential. With dedication and targeted improvements, you can enhance your prospects for success in the future. Keep striving, and don't lose hope.
